1: Samuel is sent to Bethlehem (bêt lāḥem, 'house of bread') to anoint one of Jesse's sons. Bethlehem was an Ephrathite clan town in the hill country of Judah — the same town identified as the birthplace of David in Ruth 1:1-2, and later as the birthplace of the Messiah in Micah 5:2. Archaeological surveys of the Bethlehem area have confirmed it as an occupied site throughout the Bronze and Iron Ages, though no major excavation has been undertaken beneath the modern town. Its identity as the 'city of David' is confirmed by Luke 2:4 and is consistent with the broader geographical picture of the Judahite hill country settlements. The Ephrathite designation (cf. 1 Samuel 17:12: Jesse the Ephrathite) marks the family's connection to the ancient Judahite clan of Ephrath (cf. Genesis 35:19; Ruth 4:11).
7: YHWH's statement to Samuel — lōʾ yirʾeh hāʾāḏām ʾăšer yirʾeh ʾelōhîm kî hāʾāḏām yirʾeh lāʿênayim waYHWH yirʾeh lallēḇāḇ — 'man does not see as God sees; man looks at the outward appearance (ʿênayim, eyes) and YHWH looks at the heart (lēḇāḇ)' — is the anointing narrative's theological thesis. The reversal of human evaluation is the consistent OT pattern: the last becomes first, the youngest becomes king, the shepherd becomes the anointed. In ANE coronation ideology, the king was typically the tallest, the most impressive, the most militarily dominant — the very description of Saul (1 Samuel 9:2). David's election inverts this: he is the youngest, absent, tending the sheep. The election of the unlikely is not merely God's preference but the structural demonstration that the king rules by divine choice rather than human qualification — which is what distinguishes Israelite from Mesopotamian royal ideology.
13: The anointing ritual: Samuel takes the horn of oil (qeren haššemen) and anoints David in the midst of his brothers. The anointing with oil is attested in ANE coronation ceremonies from Egypt (where Pharaoh anointed vassal kings), to Ugarit, to the Hittite court. In Israel, the anointing functioned differently: it was a priestly-prophetic act that designated YHWH's chosen rather than a royal display of power. The immediate consequence — 'the Spirit of YHWH rushed upon David from that day forward' (ṣālaḥ rûaḥ YHWH ʾel dāwiḏ mēhayyôm hahûʾ wāmāʿlāh) — makes explicit the connection between the anointing and Spirit-empowerment. Three elements converge: the oil (material symbol), the prophetic word (Samuel's act is authoritative because Samuel is YHWH's prophet), and the Spirit (the effective power). The same three-element pattern appears at Jesus's baptism: water (the material), the divine voice (the word of authorization), and the Spirit (the effective anointing). Luke 4:18 cites Isaiah 61:1 — 'The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me' — as Jesus's self-identification as the ultimate anointed one whose Spirit-empowerment is permanent rather than occasional.
14: The departure of the Spirit from Saul is one of the most theologically challenging verses in the Samuel narrative: wərûaḥ YHWH sārāh mēʿim šāʾûl ûbiʿăṯāṯû rûaḥ rāʿāh mēʾēt YHWH — 'the Spirit of YHWH departed from Saul, and a harmful spirit from YHWH tormented him.' The phrase rûaḥ rāʿāh mēʾēt YHWH (a harmful spirit from YHWH) raises questions about divine agency in distressing states. OT theology consistently subordinates all spiritual powers — including malevolent ones — to YHWH's sovereign will (cf. Job 1-2, where the adversary operates only within divinely permitted limits). The harmful spirit here is understood as YHWH's withdrawal of the Spirit's protective presence, leaving Saul vulnerable to spiritual distress. David's harp music (kinnôr) as the remedy introduces the therapeutic-liturgical dimension: music as the vehicle of spiritual relief anticipates the Psalms' role in Israel's spiritual formation. The image of the Spirit departing from Saul and resting permanently on David is the visual narrative of the covenant transition.
2: Samuel's objection: 'How can I go? If Saul hears it, he will kill me.' Samuel's fear reveals the political danger of anointing a new king while Saul lives. The threat of royal execution for prophets is attested in the Elijah-Jezebel narratives (1 Kings 19:2) and in Jeremiah's experience (Jeremiah 26:20-23). YHWH's response — take a heifer as a cover story for visiting Bethlehem for a sacrifice — is a form of protective concealment, raising interesting questions about deception for protective purposes that Jewish and Christian interpreters have long discussed.
3: YHWH's instructions to Samuel at Bethlehem: 'Invite Jesse to the sacrifice, and I will show you what you shall do. And you shall anoint for me him whom I declare to you.' The withholding of the specific name until the moment of selection — YHWH will reveal the anointed one at the sacrifice, not in advance — maintains the narrative suspense and establishes that the selection is entirely YHWH's. The formula wᵉʾet ʾăšer ʾōmar ʾēlêḵā tāšûr (the one whom I tell you, him you shall anoint) is the prophetic commission in its most concise form.
4: Samuel came to Bethlehem, and the elders of the city came to meet him trembling. The trembling (wayeḥᵉrᵉḏû) of the Bethlehem elders at Samuel's arrival reflects his dual role as prophet and judge: prophetic visitations could mean both blessing and judgment. Samuel's reassurance — 'I come peacefully; I have come to sacrifice to YHWH' — uses the peace formula (šālôm) that distinguishes a visit of blessing from one of judgment. The elders' fear and Samuel's reassurance establish the social setting of the anointing.
5: Samuel consecrated Jesse and his sons and invited them to the sacrifice. The consecration (qiddēš) required ritual preparation — washing, changing garments, abstaining from sexual relations (cf. Exodus 19:10-15 for the Sinai paradigm) — before approaching YHWH in sacrifice. The invitation of Jesse's household to the sacrificial meal provides the cover within which the anointing will occur. The sequence: public sacrifice (cover story), private divine selection (the real purpose).
6: When they came, Samuel saw Eliab and thought, 'Surely the LORD's anointed is before him.' Samuel's first impression — that Eliab, the eldest son, must be the one — is the human default toward primogeniture and physical impression. Eliab is apparently physically impressive (implied by the correction in v7). Samuel's error here is the error of every human observer who sees with external eyes: the height and appearance that initially recommended Saul (1 Samuel 9:2: 'there was not a man among the people of Israel more handsome than he') misled Samuel then too.
8: Abinadab passed before Samuel and Samuel said, 'Neither has YHWH chosen this one.' The second rejection. The narrative economy of the seven rejections — each noted briefly — creates a rhythmic pattern that builds anticipation for the eighth. The formula 'YHWH has not chosen this one' (gam bāzeh lōʾ bāḥar YHWH) is the divine negative that will be reversed only when the unexpected seventh/eighth son is presented.
9: Shammah passed and Samuel said, 'Neither has YHWH chosen this one.' The third rejection. The enumeration of Jesse's sons in 1 Chronicles 2:13-15 lists David as the seventh son (not eighth as sometimes calculated here); the difference may reflect a son who died without issue. The sequential review of the sons before their father and the prophet gives the scene the character of a formal presentation — a court introduction, almost a parade of candidates.
10: Jesse made seven of his sons pass before Samuel, and Samuel said to Jesse, 'YHWH has not chosen these.' The summary of seven rejections before the question about remaining sons establishes the contrast: YHWH's choice will not be among the expected candidates. The number seven is complete; the next will be beyond the expected sequence. The pattern of election beyond the expected order — younger over older — runs through Israel's narrative (Isaac over Ishmael, Jacob over Esau, Ephraim over Manasseh, Judah over Reuben).
11: Samuel's question: 'Are all your sons here?' Jesse's answer: 'There remains yet the youngest, but behold, he is keeping the sheep.' The word haqqāṭān (the youngest/smallest) echoes Samuel's earlier address to Saul about being 'small in your own eyes' (v15:17) and Benjamin being 'the smallest tribe.' The youngest, tending sheep — the most marginalized member of the family, given the task appropriate for the youngest and least — is the one YHWH has chosen. Samuel's response: 'Send and get him, for we will not sit down till he comes here.'
12: The description of David: 'He was ruddy (ʾadmônî), and had beautiful eyes (yᵉpēh ʿênayim), and was handsome (ṭôḇ rōʾî).' The three physical descriptors — ruddy complexion, beautiful eyes, handsome appearance — ironically describe physical attractiveness despite the principle just stated in v7 (YHWH looks at the heart, not outward appearance). The resolution: YHWH is not anti-physical beauty; the point of v7 is that physical appearance is not the criterion of election, not that it is absent from the chosen. David is handsome, but he is not chosen for that.
15: Saul's servants describe the troubled spirit and propose a solution: 'Let our lord command your servants who are before you to seek out a man who is skillful in playing the lyre, and when the evil spirit from God is upon you, he will play it, and you will be well.' The therapeutic use of music — specifically stringed instruments — as treatment for emotional or spiritual disturbance is attested in ancient Near Eastern medical texts. The Mesopotamian tradition included 'incantation priests' who combined music and verbal formulas for healing; the Israelite practice here is stripped of incantation but retains the music therapy element.
16: Saul's agreement: 'Provide for me a man who can play well and bring him to me.' The king's receptivity to the musical cure is the narrative mechanism that brings David to court. The request is for musical skill (yōdēaʿ mᵉnaggēn, one who knows how to play), not for spiritual insight or prophetic gift. Saul in his degraded state needs a musician; YHWH in his providence provides the one on whom the Spirit rests — more than Saul asked for.
17: Saul to his servants: 'Provide for me a man who can play well and bring him to me.' The king's court required skilled musicians: ancient Near Eastern royal courts, including Assyrian and Egyptian ones, maintained professional musicians among their staff. The court musician was a recognized social role; David's entry into Saul's service as a musician is consistent with ANE court practice.
18: One of the young men described David: 'I have seen a son of Jesse the Bethlehemite, who is skillful in playing, a man of valor, a man of war, prudent in speech, and a man of good presence, and YHWH is with him.' The sixfold description is remarkable: musical skill + military valor + combat experience + wisdom in speech + good appearance + divine presence. The accumulation of qualities presents David as the complete leader. The final qualification — 'YHWH is with him' (waYHWH ʿimmô) — is the theological summary that distinguishes David from all previous candidates.
19: Saul sent messengers to Jesse: 'Send me David your son, who is with the sheep.' The identification of David through his pastoral occupation — with the sheep — connects the anointing narrative with the call narratives of other leaders who were working when called (Moses tending Jethro's flock at the burning bush, Exodus 3:1; Elisha plowing with 12 yoke of oxen when Elijah called him, 1 Kings 19:19-20). The shepherd who becomes king is a topos in ancient Near Eastern royal ideology; Hammurabi calls himself the 'shepherd of the people' in his law code prologue.
20: Jesse sent David to Saul with a donkey loaded with bread, a skin of wine, and a young goat as a gift. The gift (minḥāh) accompanying a request for royal service follows ANE protocol for approaching a king or great man (cf. 1 Samuel 9:7-8, Saul's gift to Samuel). The specific provisions — bread, wine, goat — are the standard components of a formal gift presentation. Jesse's generous gift reflects the social honor of his son being requested for royal service.
21: David came to Saul and entered his service. Saul loved him greatly, and David became his armor-bearer. The office of armor-bearer (nōśēʾ kēlîm, carrier of weapons) was a position of trust and proximity to the king in battle — the armor-bearer fought alongside and protected the king. Jonathan's armor-bearer famously fought with him at Michmash (1 Samuel 14:6-14); Saul's armor-bearer refused to kill his master at Gilboa (1 Samuel 31:4-5). The office is an honorable military appointment that places David within arm's reach of the king.
22: Saul sent to Jesse: 'Let David remain in my service, for he has found favor in my sight.' The favor-formula (māṣāʾ ḥēn bᵉʿênay) is the standard phrase for royal approval of a courtier (cf. Genesis 39:4, Joseph finding favor in Potiphar's sight; Esther 2:9, Esther finding favor in the house-warden's sight). Saul's official request to retain David constitutes a formal appointment to royal service. Jesse's son has gone from the pasture to the palace through the chain of providential events set in motion by the anointing.
23: When the evil spirit from God was upon Saul, David took the lyre and played it with his hand. Saul would be refreshed and well, and the evil spirit would depart from him. The efficacy of David's music in relieving Saul's distress is presented without rationalistic explanation: the narrative simply reports the effect. The therapeutic function of music in antiquity is well-attested (the Egyptian papyrus Ebers recommends vocal incantations for healing; Assyrian exorcism rituals used musical accompaniment); what is distinctive here is that the benefit comes through the one on whom the Spirit of YHWH rests — the Spirit-anointed musician brings rûaḥ-relief to the Spirit-abandoned king.
